title: Networking
description: Retrofit, OkHttp, serialization, API patterns, GraphQL, retry policies, and real-time data
lesson:
Networking in Android

Retrofit is the standard HTTP client for Android. It turns your API into a Kotlin interface with annotations. OkHttp handles the actual HTTP calls underneath. Together they form the backbone of nearly every Android app's networking layer.

Android Networking Stack: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Your Code (ViewModel / Repository) │ Retrofit ←── Interface + Annotations → Type-safe API calls │ OkHttp ←── Interceptors, TLS, Connection pooling, HTTP/2 │ Socket / TLS │ Network (WiFi / Cellular) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Retrofit Setup & Interface Declaration

Retrofit uses annotations on a Kotlin interface to describe HTTP requests. Each method maps to one endpoint. With suspend functions, Retrofit automatically performs the call on a background thread (via OkHttp's dispatcher) and resumes on the calling coroutine's dispatcher.

// Define API interface with suspend functions interface UserApi { @GET("users/{id}") suspend fun getUser(@Path("id") id: String): User @GET("users") suspend fun getUsers( @Query("page") page: Int, @Query("limit") limit: Int = 20 ): List<User> @POST("users") suspend fun createUser(@Body user: User): User @PUT("users/{id}") suspend fun updateUser( @Path("id") id: String, @Body user: User ): User @DELETE("users/{id}") suspend fun deleteUser(@Path("id") id: String) @Multipart @POST("users/{id}/avatar") suspend fun uploadAvatar( @Path("id") id: String, @Part image: MultipartBody.Part ): User @Headers("Cache-Control: no-cache") @GET("users/{id}/profile") suspend fun getProfile(@Path("id") id: String): Profile // Dynamic headers via @HeaderMap @GET("users") suspend fun getUsersWithHeaders( @HeaderMap headers: Map<String, String> ): List<User> }

Retrofit Converters & Adapters

Converters tell Retrofit how to serialize/deserialize request and response bodies. You must add exactly one converter factory. If you add multiple, Retrofit tries them in order and uses the first one that can handle the type.

// kotlinx.serialization converter (recommended) val json = Json { ignoreUnknownKeys = true // don't crash on extra JSON fields isLenient = true // accept malformed JSON encodeDefaults = false // skip default values in serialization coerceInputValues = true // coerce nulls to defaults } val retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .client(okHttpClient) .addConverterFactory(json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() // Moshi converter (alternative) val moshi = Moshi.Builder() .addLast(KotlinJsonAdapterFactory()) .build() val retrofitMoshi = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .addConverterFactory(MoshiConverterFactory.create(moshi)) .build() // Gson converter (legacy — not recommended for new code) val retrofitGson = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .addConverterFactory(GsonConverterFactory.create()) .build() val api = retrofit.create(UserApi::class.java)
Return type matters:
suspend fun getUser(): User — Retrofit deserializes the body directly. Throws HttpException on non-2xx.
suspend fun getUser(): Response<User> — You get the full response, including headers and status code. Never throws on HTTP errors.
Choose Response<T> when you need headers or status codes; choose T directly when you want cleaner code with exception-based error handling.

OkHttp Interceptors

Interceptors are middleware that sit in the request/response pipeline. They can modify, log, retry, or cache requests. OkHttp supports two types: application interceptors and network interceptors, which differ in when they run relative to the cache and retry logic.

OkHttp Interceptor Chain: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Application Code │ App Interceptor 1 (Auth) ← addInterceptor() │ App Interceptor 2 (Logging) ← addInterceptor() │ OkHttp Core (retries, redirects, cache) │ Network Interceptor 1 (Cache) ← addNetworkInterceptor() │ Network (actual HTTP call) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ App interceptors: Called ONCE, even with retries/redirects Network interceptors: Called for EACH network call (including redirects)
// Auth token interceptor — adds Bearer token to every request class AuthInterceptor(private val tokenProvider: TokenProvider) : Interceptor { override fun intercept(chain: Interceptor.Chain): Response { val request = chain.request().newBuilder() .header("Authorization", "Bearer ${tokenProvider.getToken()}") .build() return chain.proceed(request) } } // Token refresh interceptor — handles 401 by refreshing the token class TokenRefreshInterceptor( private val tokenProvider: TokenProvider ) : Interceptor { override fun intercept(chain: Interceptor.Chain): Response { val request = chain.request() val response = chain.proceed(request) if (response.code == 401) { response.close() // Refresh the token synchronously val newToken = tokenProvider.refreshTokenSync() val newRequest = request.newBuilder() .header("Authorization", "Bearer $newToken") .build() return chain.proceed(newRequest) } return response } } // Retry interceptor — retries failed requests with backoff class RetryInterceptor(private val maxRetries: Int = 3) : Interceptor { override fun intercept(chain: Interceptor.Chain): Response { var lastException: IOException? = null repeat(maxRetries) { attempt -> try { val response = chain.proceed(chain.request()) if (response.isSuccessful || response.code !in 500..599) { return response } response.close() // close before retry } catch (e: IOException) { lastException = e } Thread.sleep((1000L * (attempt + 1))) // linear backoff } throw lastException ?: IOException("Request failed after $maxRetries retries") } } // Logging interceptor — logs request/response for debugging val loggingInterceptor = HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply { level = HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY // NONE in production! } // Build OkHttpClient with interceptors chained val okHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor(tokenProvider)) // app-level: auth .addInterceptor(TokenRefreshInterceptor(provider)) // app-level: 401 refresh .addInterceptor(RetryInterceptor(maxRetries = 3)) // app-level: retry .addInterceptor(loggingInterceptor) // app-level: logging .addNetworkInterceptor(CacheInterceptor()) // network-level: cache .connectTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .readTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .writeTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .build()
Interceptor order matters! Interceptors run in the order you add them. Put auth before logging so the logged request includes the Authorization header. Put retry after auth so retried requests also include the token.
App interceptors vs Network interceptors:
App interceptors (addInterceptor): Called once per request, even with retries/redirects. Use for: auth headers, logging, retry logic.
Network interceptors (addNetworkInterceptor): Called for each network call (including redirects). Use for: cache headers, compression, response rewriting.
Logging tip: Use Level.BODY only in debug builds. In production, use Level.NONE or Level.BASIC to avoid logging sensitive data and wasting memory on large response bodies.

Certificate Pinning

Certificate pinning prevents man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks by verifying that the server's certificate matches a known hash. Without pinning, any CA-signed certificate is trusted — an attacker with a rogue CA cert could intercept your traffic.

Certificate Pinning — Why It Matters: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Without pinning: App ──→ Attacker (rogue CA cert) ──→ Real Server ✗ App trusts any CA — attacker can intercept With pinning: App ──→ Attacker (wrong cert hash) ──✗ REJECTED App ──→ Real Server (pinned hash) ──✓ ACCEPTED ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ What to pin: Leaf cert — Most specific, breaks on rotation Intermediate CA — Good balance of security and durability Root CA — Least specific, survives leaf rotation
// Pin certificates to prevent MITM attacks val certificatePinner = CertificatePinner.Builder() .add("api.example.com", "sha256/AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA=") .add("api.example.com", "sha256/BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB=") // backup pin .build() val client = OkHttpClient.Builder() .certificatePinner(certificatePinner) .build() // To get a server's certificate hash, run: // openssl s_client -connect api.example.com:443 | \ // openssl x509 -pubkey -noout | \ // openssl pkey -pubin -outform der | \ // openssl dgst -sha256 -binary | openssl enc -base64
Always include a backup pin! If your primary certificate rotates and you only pinned one hash, your app will stop working until you ship an update. Pin at least two certificates (current + backup/CA). Consider using Android Network Security Config (res/xml/network_security_config.xml) for a declarative approach that supports per-domain pins with expiration dates.
<!-- res/xml/network_security_config.xml — declarative pinning --> <network-security-config> <domain-config> <domain includeSubdomains="true">api.example.com</domain> <pin-set expiration="2025-06-01"> <pin digest="SHA-256">AAAA...</pin> <pin digest="SHA-256">BBBB...</pin> </pin-set> </domain-config> </network-security-config>
Network Security Config advantage: If your pins expire (past the expiration date), Android gracefully falls back to normal certificate validation instead of hard-failing. This is safer than OkHttp-only pinning where expired pins cause crashes in production.

Serialization: JSON Libraries Compared

Choosing the right JSON library affects performance, null safety, and Kotlin compatibility. Here is a detailed comparison of the three major options:

JSON Serialization Libraries: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ kotlinx.serialization Moshi Gson ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Kotlin-first Yes (compiler plugin) Yes No (Java) Code generation Compile-time Compile-time Runtime reflection Null safety Enforced at compile Enforced Silently assigns null Performance Fastest (no reflection) Fast (codegen) Slowest (reflection) Default values Respected Respected Ignored (overwritten) Sealed classes Built-in polymorphism Custom adapter Manual type handling Multiplatform Yes (KMP/iOS/JS/WASM) JVM only JVM only Streaming parser No Yes (JsonReader) Yes (JsonReader) Custom adapters Serializer interface JsonAdapter TypeAdapter ProGuard/R8 Minimal rules needed Moderate Many keep rules ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Recommendation: kotlinx.serialization → new Kotlin projects, KMP Moshi → existing projects, Java interop needed Gson → legacy only (migrate away when possible)
// ═══ kotlinx.serialization ═══ @Serializable data class Article( val id: Int, val title: String, @SerialName("published_at") val publishedAt: String, val tags: List<String> = emptyList() // default if missing from JSON ) // Polymorphic sealed classes — built-in! @Serializable sealed class ApiEvent { @Serializable @SerialName("message") data class Message(val text: String, val sender: String) : ApiEvent() @Serializable @SerialName("typing") data class Typing(val userId: String) : ApiEvent() } // Configure the Json instance val json = Json { ignoreUnknownKeys = true // API adds new fields? No crash. coerceInputValues = true // null for non-null field? Use default. encodeDefaults = false // skip defaults when serializing } // ═══ Moshi ═══ @JsonClass(generateAdapter = true) data class ArticleMoshi( val id: Int, val title: String, @Json(name = "published_at") val publishedAt: String, val tags: List<String> = emptyList() ) // ═══ Gson (legacy) ═══ data class ArticleGson( val id: Int, val title: String, @SerializedName("published_at") val publishedAt: String, val tags: List<String> = emptyList() // WARNING: Gson ignores this default! )
Gson's null safety problem: Gson uses reflection and bypasses Kotlin constructors. If a JSON field is missing, Gson assigns null to the property — even if the property is declared non-null (val name: String). This causes NullPointerException at runtime, defeating Kotlin's null safety. kotlinx.serialization and Moshi both respect Kotlin's type system and will throw at deserialization time if a non-null field is null.

Error Handling — Sealed Result Class

Never use response.body()!!. Always wrap network calls with proper error handling. A sealed result type gives you exhaustive when expressions and makes error states explicit in the type system.

// Sealed class for API results — Loading state included sealed class ApiResult<out T> { data class Success<T>(val data: T) : ApiResult<T>() data class Error( val message: String, val code: Int? = null, val throwable: Throwable? = null ) : ApiResult<Nothing>() data object Loading : ApiResult<Nothing>() } // Generic safe API call wrapper suspend fun <T> safeApiCall(block: suspend () -> T): ApiResult<T> { return try { ApiResult.Success(block()) } catch (e: HttpException) { // HTTP error (4xx, 5xx) — server responded with error val errorBody = e.response()?.errorBody()?.string() ApiResult.Error( message = parseErrorMessage(errorBody) ?: e.message(), code = e.code(), throwable = e ) } catch (e: IOException) { // Network error — no internet, DNS failure, timeout ApiResult.Error( message = "Check your internet connection", throwable = e ) } catch (e: SerializationException) { // JSON parsing error — API changed shape unexpectedly ApiResult.Error( message = "Data format error", throwable = e ) } } // Parse server error body (typically {"error": "message"}) private fun parseErrorMessage(errorBody: String?): String? { return try { errorBody?.let { Json.decodeFromString<ErrorResponse>(it).message } } catch (_: Exception) { null } } // Usage in Repository class UserRepository(private val api: UserApi) { suspend fun getUser(id: String): ApiResult<User> = safeApiCall { api.getUser(id) } // Flow-based for reactive UI updates fun getUserFlow(id: String): Flow<ApiResult<User>> = flow { emit(ApiResult.Loading) emit(safeApiCall { api.getUser(id) }) } } // Usage in ViewModel — exhaustive when class UserViewModel(private val repo: UserRepository) : ViewModel() { private val _state = MutableStateFlow<ApiResult<User>>(ApiResult.Loading) val state: StateFlow<ApiResult<User>> = _state fun loadUser(id: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _state.value = ApiResult.Loading _state.value = repo.getUser(id) } } } // Compose UI with exhaustive state handling @Composable fun UserScreen(viewModel: UserViewModel) { when (val state = viewModel.state.collectAsStateWithLifecycle().value) { is ApiResult.Loading -> CircularProgressIndicator() is ApiResult.Success -> UserCard(state.data) is ApiResult.Error -> ErrorMessage(state.message) } }
HttpException vs IOException — interview favorite:
HttpException — Server responded, but with an error status (404, 401, 500). You have a response body with error details. The network is fine; the request failed.
IOException — No response received at all. Network down, DNS failure, connection timeout, socket closed. No error body available.
SerializationException — Response received and HTTP was 200, but the JSON body could not be parsed into your data class. Common when the API changes shape without versioning.

Handle all three to build robust apps. Most interview candidates miss SerializationException.
Never use response.body()!! — this is the most common Retrofit mistake. If the server returns a 204 No Content or an unexpected shape, body() returns null and !! crashes your app. Always use the sealed result pattern or check for null.

Pagination with Paging 3

Paging 3 handles infinite scrolling lists efficiently — loading data in pages, caching, showing loading/error states, and supporting both network-only and offline-first patterns. It is composed of three layers: the data layer (PagingSource / RemoteMediator), the domain layer (Pager), and the UI layer (LazyPagingItems).

Paging 3 Architecture — Two Patterns: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Pattern 1: Network Only (simple) PagingSource ─→ Pager ─→ Flow<PagingData> ─→ LazyPagingItems (loads from API) (config) (cached in VM) (Compose UI) Pattern 2: Offline-First (RemoteMediator) RemoteMediator ──→ Network API │ │ │ fetched data │ │ └──────→ Room DB ←─┘ │ PagingSource ─→ Pager ─→ Flow<PagingData> ─→ UI (reads from DB) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Key: RemoteMediator handles the network fetch + DB insert. PagingSource reads only from Room (single source of truth). UI always shows data from Room — works offline.
// ═══ Pattern 1: PagingSource (Network Only) ═══ class ArticlePagingSource( private val api: ArticleApi ) : PagingSource<Int, Article>() { override suspend fun load(params: LoadParams<Int>): LoadResult<Int, Article> { val page = params.key ?: 1 return try { val response = api.getArticles(page = page, limit = params.loadSize) LoadResult.Page( data = response, prevKey = if (page == 1) null else page - 1, nextKey = if (response.isEmpty()) null else page + 1 ) } catch (e: Exception) { LoadResult.Error(e) } } override fun getRefreshKey(state: PagingState<Int, Article>): Int? { return state.anchorPosition?.let { anchor -> state.closestPageToPosition(anchor)?.prevKey?.plus(1) } } } // ═══ Pattern 2: RemoteMediator (Offline-First) ═══ @OptIn(ExperimentalPagingApi::class) class ArticleRemoteMediator( private val api: ArticleApi, private val db: AppDatabase ) : RemoteMediator<Int, ArticleEntity>() { override suspend fun load( loadType: LoadType, state: PagingState<Int, ArticleEntity> ): MediatorResult { val page = when (loadType) { LoadType.REFRESH -> 1 LoadType.PREPEND -> return MediatorResult.Success( endOfPaginationReached = true ) LoadType.APPEND -> { val lastItem = state.lastItemOrNull() ?: return MediatorResult.Success( endOfPaginationReached = true ) lastItem.nextPage } } return try { val articles = api.getArticles( page = page, limit = state.config.pageSize ) db.withTransaction { if (loadType == LoadType.REFRESH) { db.articleDao().clearAll() } db.articleDao().insertAll( articles.map { it.toEntity(nextPage = page + 1) } ) } MediatorResult.Success( endOfPaginationReached = articles.isEmpty() ) } catch (e: Exception) { MediatorResult.Error(e) } } } // ═══ ViewModel — Pager Configuration ═══ class ArticleViewModel( private val api: ArticleApi, private val db: AppDatabase ) : ViewModel() { // Network only val articlesNetworkOnly: Flow<PagingData<Article>> = Pager( config = PagingConfig( pageSize = 20, prefetchDistance = 5, // start loading 5 items before end initialLoadSize = 40, // load extra on first page enablePlaceholders = false // no null placeholders ) ) { ArticlePagingSource(api) }.flow.cachedIn(viewModelScope) // Offline-first with RemoteMediator @OptIn(ExperimentalPagingApi::class) val articlesOfflineFirst: Flow<PagingData<ArticleEntity>> = Pager( config = PagingConfig(pageSize = 20), remoteMediator = ArticleRemoteMediator(api, db), pagingSourceFactory = { db.articleDao().pagingSource() } ).flow.cachedIn(viewModelScope) } // ═══ Compose UI — LazyPagingItems ═══ @Composable fun ArticleList(viewModel: ArticleViewModel) { val articles = viewModel.articlesNetworkOnly.collectAsLazyPagingItems() LazyColumn { // Main content items( count = articles.itemCount, key = articles.itemKey { it.id } ) { index -> articles[index]?.let { ArticleCard(it) } } // Append loading indicator when (articles.loadState.append) { is LoadState.Loading -> { item { CircularProgressIndicator(Modifier.fillMaxWidth()) } } is LoadState.Error -> { item { RetryButton(onClick = { articles.retry() }) } } else -> {} } } // Full-screen loading on initial load if (articles.loadState.refresh is LoadState.Loading) { FullScreenLoader() } // Pull-to-refresh PullToRefreshBox( isRefreshing = articles.loadState.refresh is LoadState.Loading, onRefresh = { articles.refresh() } ) { /* LazyColumn content */ } }
PagingConfig parameters explained:
pageSize — Number of items per page. Match your API's page size.
prefetchDistance — How many items from the end to trigger the next page load. Default is pageSize. Lower = less aggressive, higher = smoother scrolling.
initialLoadSize — Items to load on the first request. Default is 3 * pageSize. Increase for faster initial screen fill.
enablePlaceholders — If true, unloaded items appear as null (useful for fixed-size lists). Set false for most infinite scroll use cases.
cachedIn(viewModelScope) — Caches the PagingData in the ViewModel so configuration changes (rotation) don't re-fetch.
RemoteMediator gotcha: Always wrap the database insert in db.withTransaction. If the clear and insert are not atomic, the user briefly sees an empty list during refresh. Also remember to return MediatorResult.Success(endOfPaginationReached = true) for PREPEND if you only paginate forward.

GraphQL with Apollo-Android

GraphQL lets clients request exactly the data they need — no over-fetching or under-fetching:

RESTGraphQL
Fixed response shapeClient specifies fields
Multiple endpointsSingle endpoint
Over/under-fetching commonExact data requested
Simpler caching (HTTP cache)Normalized cache (by ID)
// 1. Define GraphQL query in .graphql file: // query GetUser($id: ID!) { // user(id: $id) { // id // name // email // posts { title } // } // } // 2. Apollo generates type-safe Kotlin code val apolloClient = ApolloClient.Builder() .serverUrl("https://api.example.com/graphql") .normalizedCache(MemoryCacheFactory(maxSizeBytes = 10 * 1024 * 1024)) .build() // 3. Execute query val response = apolloClient.query( GetUserQuery(id = "123") ).execute() val user = response.data?.user // 4. Execute mutation val mutation = apolloClient.mutation( UpdateUserMutation(id = "123", name = "Alice") ).execute()

Retry Policies

// Exponential backoff with jitter suspend fun <T> retryWithBackoff( maxRetries: Int = 3, initialDelay: Long = 1000, maxDelay: Long = 16000, factor: Double = 2.0, block: suspend () -> T ): T { var currentDelay = initialDelay repeat(maxRetries - 1) { try { return block() } catch (e: IOException) { delay(currentDelay + Random.nextLong(0, currentDelay / 2)) // jitter currentDelay = (currentDelay * factor).toLong().coerceAtMost(maxDelay) } } return block() // Final attempt — let exception propagate }
Jitter adds randomness to prevent the thundering herd problem — without it, all retrying clients hit the server at exactly the same time.

Real-Time Data

  • WebSocket — Full-duplex, persistent connection. Use for: chat, live scores, collaborative editing. OkHttp has built-in WebSocket support.
  • SSE (Server-Sent Events) — Server pushes events over HTTP. One-directional (server → client). Simpler than WebSocket for notifications/feeds.
  • Firebase Realtime DB / Firestore — Managed real-time sync with offline support.

Dependency Injection for Networking

In production apps, networking components are provided via DI (Hilt/Dagger). This enables testing with fake APIs and swapping base URLs per build variant:

// Hilt module providing networking dependencies @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideOkHttpClient( authInterceptor: AuthInterceptor ): OkHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder() .addInterceptor(authInterceptor) .connectTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .build() @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(client: OkHttpClient): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl(BuildConfig.API_BASE_URL) // different per build variant .client(client) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() @Provides @Singleton fun provideUserApi(retrofit: Retrofit): UserApi = retrofit.create(UserApi::class.java) }

Network Testing Strategies

Testing networking code requires isolating the HTTP layer. The two main approaches are MockWebServer (integration tests) and fake implementations (unit tests):

// ═══ MockWebServer (integration test) ═══ class UserApiTest { private val mockWebServer = MockWebServer() private lateinit var api: UserApi @Before fun setup() { mockWebServer.start() api = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl(mockWebServer.url("/")) .addConverterFactory( Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType()) ) .build() .create(UserApi::class.java) } @Test fun getUser_returns_user_on_200() = runTest { mockWebServer.enqueue(MockResponse() .setBody("{\"id\":\"1\",\"name\":\"Alice\"}") .setResponseCode(200)) val user = api.getUser("1") assertEquals("Alice", user.name) } @Test fun getUser_throws_on_404() = runTest { mockWebServer.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(404)) assertThrows<HttpException> { api.getUser("1") } } @After fun teardown() = mockWebServer.shutdown() } // ═══ Fake API (unit test — faster, no server) ═══ class FakeUserApi : UserApi { var shouldFail = false override suspend fun getUser(id: String): User { if (shouldFail) throw IOException("Fake network error") return User(id = id, name = "Test User") } // ... implement other methods }
When to use which:
MockWebServer — Tests the full Retrofit + OkHttp stack (serialization, interceptors, headers). Slower, but catches integration bugs.
Fake API — Tests your Repository/ViewModel logic without any HTTP. Fast, but does not exercise serialization or interceptors.

questions.title: Retrofit Interface
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define a Retrofit API interface.
questions.explanation: Retrofit uses annotations to define HTTP APIs. @GET/@POST specify HTTP methods. @Path substitutes URL path variables. @Body serializes the parameter as request body. suspend makes them coroutine-friendly.
hints: The four most common HTTP methods are GET, POST, PUT, DELETE

questions.title: Network on Main Thread
questions.description: This code causes the app to freeze (ANR). Fix it.

checks.message: Use coroutines to move network call off the main thread

questions.explanation: Network calls on the main thread block the UI, causing ANR after 5 seconds. Wrap in viewModelScope.launch. With Retrofit's suspend functions, it automatically switches to a background thread.
hints: What scope should you use for coroutines in a ViewModel?

questions.title: OkHttp Interceptors
questions.description: What is the primary use case for OkHttp Interceptors?
options: To parse JSON responses
To intercept and modify HTTP requests/responses (add headers, log, retry, cache)
To handle deep links
To encrypt traffic with SSL
questions.explanation: Interceptors sit in the request/response pipeline. Common uses: auth interceptor (adds Bearer token), logging interceptor, cache interceptor, retry interceptor. They chain together for clean separation of concerns.
hints: Think middleware — sitting between your code and the network

questions.title: API Error Handling
questions.description: This code has no error handling. Add proper error handling with a sealed Result type.
questions.explanation: The original crashes on null body (!!) and has no handling for network failures or HTTP errors. A proper solution: try/catch for network exceptions, check isSuccessful for HTTP errors, handle null body, and return a sealed type.
hints: What happens if the network is off? Or the server returns 500?

questions.title: kotlinx.serialization
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define a serializable data class for JSON parsing.

blanks.hint: Maps JSON key to Kotlin property name

questions.explanation: @Serializable marks a class for kotlinx.serialization. @SerialName maps JSON field names to Kotlin property names when they differ (snake_case → camelCase). Default values handle missing fields gracefully.
hints: The annotation tells the compiler to generate serialization code
JSON APIs often use snake_case, but Kotlin uses camelCase

questions.title: Network Connectivity Observer
questions.description: Write a function that observes network connectivity changes using ConnectivityManager and returns a Flow<Boolean>.

checks.message: Clean up the callback when the flow is cancelled

questions.explanation: callbackFlow bridges callback-based APIs to Kotlin Flows. Register the callback, emit values with trySend(), and unregister in awaitClose. This creates a reactive stream of connectivity state that cleans up automatically when the collector stops.
hints: callbackFlow bridges callback-based APIs to Flow
Always clean up in awaitClose to prevent leaks

questions.title: Cache Interceptor
questions.description: What does this OkHttp interceptor do?
options: It blocks all network requests and serves only cached data
It adds a Cache-Control header to responses, telling OkHttp to cache them for 5 minutes (300 seconds)
It encrypts the response before caching
It retries failed requests up to 300 times
questions.explanation: This interceptor modifies API responses to add a Cache-Control: public, max-age=300 header. This tells OkHttp's cache to store the response for 300 seconds (5 minutes). During that window, identical requests are served from cache without hitting the network. It also removes the Pragma header which can interfere with caching. This is useful when the server doesn't set proper cache headers.

questions.title: REST vs GraphQL
questions.description: What is the primary advantage of GraphQL over REST for mobile clients?
options: GraphQL is faster because it uses HTTP/3
Clients request exactly the fields they need, eliminating over-fetching and reducing bandwidth on mobile networks
GraphQL doesn't require a server — it works locally
GraphQL automatically caches all responses
questions.explanation: GraphQL's killer feature for mobile: the client specifies exactly which fields it needs. A REST endpoint might return 50 fields when you only need 5 — wasting bandwidth on mobile networks. GraphQL also solves under-fetching (needing multiple REST calls to assemble one screen's data). Trade-off: GraphQL is harder to cache (no URL-based HTTP caching) and requires a normalized cache (Apollo) for good performance.
hints: Mobile networks have limited bandwidth — every byte matters
REST: server decides shape. GraphQL: client decides shape

questions.title: Exponential Backoff
questions.description: What retry delays does this produce for 3 failures?
options: 1s, 1s, 1s — constant delay
~1-1.5s, ~2-3s, then final attempt — exponential backoff with jitter
0s, 0s, 0s — immediate retries
1s, 4s, 16s — quadratic backoff
questions.explanation: First retry: delay(1000 + random(0..500)) ≈ 1-1.5s. Then delay doubles to 2000. Second retry: delay(2000 + random(0..1000)) ≈ 2-3s. Third attempt runs immediately (no catch). This is exponential backoff with jitter — delays grow exponentially to avoid overwhelming the server, and random jitter prevents all clients from retrying simultaneously (thundering herd).
hints: delay starts at 1000 and doubles each iteration
Random.nextLong adds jitter to spread out retries

questions.title: WebSocket vs SSE
questions.description: For a live sports score app that only receives updates from the server, which real-time technology is simpler and more appropriate?
options: WebSocket — it's the only option for real-time data
SSE (Server-Sent Events) — it's simpler for one-directional server-to-client updates and works over standard HTTP
Long polling — it's more efficient than both
REST with 1-second polling interval
questions.explanation: SSE is ideal for one-directional server → client updates. It works over standard HTTP (easy through proxies/firewalls), auto-reconnects, and is simpler than WebSocket. WebSocket is full-duplex (both directions) — use it for chat, collaborative editing, or gaming where the client also sends frequent messages. Polling is wasteful (constant requests even when nothing changed).
hints: The client only receives updates, never sends — which tech fits?
Full-duplex is overkill for one-directional communication
